MARKET.head: Grace Vineyard pivots to baijiu under new ownership, abandoning wine focus
MARKET.summ: Hong Kong-listed Grace Vineyard has taken another step away from its roots as one of China's best-known boutique wineries, investing in a historic baijiu producer as its new controlling shareholder accelerates plans to build a broader alcohol business
takeaways: Hong Kong-listed Grace Vineyard, China's first boutique winery to list publicly in 2018, has acquired a stake in Wang Defang Baijiu, a sauce-aroma baijiu producer based in Maotai Town, Guizhou province.
The investment follows Yang Lingjiang, founder of alcohol retailer 1919 Group, acquiring a 73.63% controlling stake in Grace Vineyard in December 2025 for approximately HK$156 million.

Grace Vineyard established six subsidiaries in Chengdu in March 2025 and plans to expand beyond wine into baijiu, beer, imported spirits and low-alcohol beverages as part of a diversified alcohol business strategy.

Industry observers question the timing and brand fit, warning that leveraging Grace Vineyard's wine reputation to market baijiu risks damaging its premium positioning as the sauce-aroma baijiu market enters a competitive contraction phase.
Wang Defang traces its origins to the late Qing Dynasty and is regarded as one of the founding brands of Ronghe Distillery, which helped establish Maotai Town as China's sauce-aroma baijiu centre.

MARKET.head: Fifteen sommeliers name pricey Bordeaux bottles worth the investment
MARKET.summ: Unlike Burgundy, where most of the region’s output is price prohibitive, Bordeaux boasts a vast number of approachable bottles with many falling within the $15 to $30 range. That said, some occasions call for a bank-breaking bottle, and Bordeaux is also well-equipped with expressions that come with three- and four-digit price tags.
takeaways: A panel of 15 US sommeliers identified expensive Bordeaux bottles they consider justified splurges, citing emotional complexity, family stewardship, and terroir over brand prestige.
Château Palmer (third growth), Château Lafleur (Pomerol), and La Mission Haut-Brion (Graves) emerged as frequently cited choices for delivering quality beyond their classification rank.
Several sommeliers favored 'Super Seconds'—second-tier estates like Château Pontet-Canet and Château Ducru-Beaucaillou—over first growths, noting comparable quality at lower price points.
Château Le Puy 'Barthélemy' and Pensées de Lafleur were highlighted as relative values: premium bottles where consumers pay for vineyard craftsmanship and family heritage rather than marketing.
Château d'Yquem, Bordeaux's flagship dessert wine, was nominated despite being outside the red-wine category, valued for three centuries of consistent production.

MARKET.head: Sicilia DOC unites fractious producers under single regional brand
MARKET.summ: Sicily isn’t Italy (you can ask anyone), and Sicilian wine isn’t Italian wine either. Many Sicilian producers have embraced the Sicilia DOC designation as a simple but effective identity. It’s the kind of idea that other wine regions might consider in today’s crowded marketplace.
takeaways: Sicily established the Sicilia DOC appellation in 2011 after years of negotiation, creating a broad regional identity to simplify consumer choice amid competing sub-regional designations like Etna DOC.
The designation struggled initially as producers prioritized their own sub-regional brands, but has grown into an important force by balancing complexity and accessibility.

Sicilia DOC has capitalized on Sicily's rising tourism profile, including The White Lotus television series effect and post-COVID travel boom, as Americans seek wines from grapes discovered on the island.
Consumers now actively hunt for indigenous Sicilian varieties—Grillo, Lucido, Nero d'Avola, Inzolia—rather than generic Italian wine, reflecting deeper engagement with the region's diverse terroir.
Producers like Tasca d'Almerita leverage Sicilia DOC to showcase Sicily's range, from Pantelleria dessert wines to Mount Etna minerality, all under a unified regional banner.

MARKET.head: China's alcohol industry group demands stricter online spirits sales regulation
MARKET.summ: China's alcohol industry has called for stronger regulation of online liquor purchases, claiming that existing rules have failed to keep pace with the rapid growth of e-commerce platforms
takeaways: The China Alcoholic Drinks Association called for tougher oversight of e-commerce platforms, citing price distortions, high commissions, and unfair competition harming producers and distributors.
The group urged regulators to enforce antitrust rules and strengthen oversight of pricing, commissions, and platform traffic allocation to level the playing field.
The association asked platforms to adopt blockchain traceability, AI consumer analytics, and supply chain systems while supporting smaller producers and cross-border expansion.
Chinese alcohol brands are valued at $123.4 billion in 2025, but IWSR forecasts a challenging 2026 with Baijiu struggling as consumers shift toward casual drinking over gifting occasions.
Consumption patterns are shifting toward younger, value-conscious drinkers embracing home and on-demand delivery, while older consumers remain cautious and selective amid economic uncertainty.

MARKET.head: Breaky Bottom, England's pioneering vineyard, listed for sale at £4 million
MARKET.summ: Breaky Bottom, one of the founding estates of the modern English wine movement, has been put up for sale for the first time since its establishment in 1974. The Sussex vineyard, created by late English wine pioneer Peter Hall, carries a guide price of £4 million
takeaways: Breaky Bottom Vineyard near Lewes, Sussex, founded by Peter Hall in 1974, has been listed for sale for the first time following Hall's death in October 2025.
The 15-acre estate, one of seven English vineyards when established, is guided at £4 million and includes six acres under vine, a farmhouse, barn winery, and production facilities.
Hall's sparkling wines from Seyval Blanc and a Chardonnay-Pinot blend were served at King Charles III's coronation, Queen Elizabeth II's Platinum Jubilee, and the 2012 London Olympics.
Knight Frank's viticulture team expects international buyer interest, citing growing demand for small-scale, terroir-driven producers despite broader wine market challenges.
The sale occurs as English wine production surged 39 percent year-on-year in 2025 and vineyard sales accelerate across the sector.

MARKET.head: Babich Wines marks 110 years as New Zealand's rare family-owned producer
MARKET.summ: From a Croatian immigrant filling barrels by hand to exports in more than 60 countries, Babich Wines' 110-year journey reflects the rise of New Zealand wine itself
takeaways: Croatian immigrant Josip Babich founded the winery in 1916 with hand-filled barrels; it now exports to 60+ countries and remains wholly family-owned across four generations.
American servicemen stationed in New Zealand during World War II bought Babich wines in U.S. dollars, providing crucial cash flow that ended decades of financial struggle.

Export sales surpassed domestic sales in 1997 after a German merchant's advance order catalyzed overseas growth; Babich now operates 14 vineyards across Marlborough and Hawke's Bay, producing 750,000 cases annually.
In 2025, David Babich's son Peter participated in his first vintage, continuing a winemaking tradition spanning over a century in an industry marked by consolidation and multinational ownership.

MARKET.head: Franzia shifts to Australian wine in China to escape 80% U.S. tariffs
MARKET.summ: Faced with nearly 80% import taxes, one of the world's best-selling boxed wine brands has abandoned U.S.-made wine in China, replacing it with Australian wine bottled locally
takeaways: Franzia, the world's largest boxed-wine brand owned by The Wine Group, now sources Australian wine for China instead of importing finished products from California.
China imposed tariffs totaling approximately 75% on U.S. wine, making the brand's original affordable pricing model unsustainable for the Chinese market.
Australian wine is now shipped to China in bulk, bottled locally, and labeled with China as country of origin—a shift from Franzia's previous direct U.S. imports.
Tao Xin, general manager of Shanghai Fuga Wines (Franzia's distributor), said local packaging offers flexibility for market-specific changes and faster response to consumer trends.
The tariff-driven restructuring mirrors Franzia's earlier bulk-import model in Japan and could reverse if U.S.-China trade policy changes.

MARKET.head: Japanese wine awards boost awareness, but small producers remain unmoved by accolades
MARKET.summ: Japanese wines have long been recognised by the likes of DWWA and IWSC, but for a country whose majority of wineries are mom-and-pop shops, do these accolades actually matter to producers looking to grow exports? Joyce Yip finds out
takeaways: Decanter World Wide Awards, International Wine & Spirit Competition, and other global competitions have recognized Japanese wines since 2010, including Suntory's 2024 Best in Show win.

Around 70 percent of Japan's 400-plus wineries are family-operated and too focused on domestic supply to pursue costly exports and award submissions, according to Kenneth Lee, a Japanese wine importer in Hong Kong.

Large producers like Château Mercian, owned by Kirin Group, and Katsunuma Jyozo have leveraged award wins—Katsunuma's 2003–2004 Vinalies medals secured a distribution deal with Bernard Magrez Group in France.
Kenneth Lee says restaurant listings and tastings now drive sales more than awards alone; exposure at high-profile events like the 2016 G7 Summit matters more than competition medals.

High production costs from Japan's mountainous terrain, humidity, and typhoons keep Japanese wines expensive overseas, remaining the primary barrier to export growth despite growing international recognition.

MARKET.head: China's top supermarkets double down on wine to reach mainstream shoppers
MARKET.summ: Here's a look at China's 10 largest supermarket chains by revenue and how each is shaping its wine strategy
takeaways: China Chain-Store & Franchise Association ranked China's top 100 supermarket operators by 2025 revenue; Walmart China led with RMB 195.86 billion, Alibaba's Freshippo second.
Walmart and Freshippo are expanding wine selection through direct sourcing and efficient supply chains to grow consumption among everyday Chinese consumers.
Costco China ranked 15th despite operating only seven stores with RMB 10 billion revenue; ALDI China jumped to 35th with 88 stores, doubling sales year-on-year.
ALDI previously offered RMB 99 six-bottle packs of imported wine bottled domestically, signaling competitive pricing strategies in the sector.
Among top 100 supermarkets, 52 reported declining revenue as traditional hypermarket models lose ground to direct-sourced products, private labels, and online-offline integration.

MARKET.head: Champagne houses mapped by flavor profile and texture
MARKET.summ: Wineries of all types strive to develop an estate identity through their liquid, but nowhere is that brand recognition as important as it is in Champagne. The flagship product for most Champagne maisons is the “house style,” a proprietary blend of grapes from assorted plots and vintages. No two non-vintage recipes are the same.
takeaways: Champagne maisons build brand identity through nonproprietary blends of Chardonnay, Pinot Noir, and Pinot Meunier in varying ratios.
Chardonnay-dominant blends taste mineral and light; Pinot Noir-heavy blends yield richer body and fruit-forward flavor.
Lees aging duration—minimum 12 months, often longer—determines final flavor: longer contact produces brioche, almond, buttery, creamy notes.
Winemakers control house style through grape selection, fermentation timing, and lees contact length before disgorgement.

CULTURE.head: Michelin launches first Burgundy winery ratings; producer immediately rejects classification
CULTURE.summ: The Michelin Guide has unveiled the inaugural Michelin Grape Selection, a new winery classification covering Burgundy. Using a system similar to its restaurant stars, Michelin awarded wineries one, two or three "Michelin Grapes" based on the overall quality of the estate rather than individual wines
takeaways: Michelin Guide unveiled its inaugural Michelin Grape Selection, rating 94 Burgundy wineries on one to three grapes based on overall estate quality rather than individual wines.
Nine estates earned three grapes, including Domaine de la Romanée-Conti, Domaine Leroy, and Domaine Cécile Tremblay; 20 received two grapes and 33 received one grape.
Domaine Arnoux-Lachaux in Vosne-Romanée publicly rejected its one-grape rating, noting it stopped submitting wines to critics in 2020 and asked Michelin for removal.
Michelin evaluates wineries using five criteria: agronomy quality, technical mastery, identity, balance and consistency, emphasizing long-term vineyard management over individual vintage scores.
The expansion marks Michelin's entry into wine ratings, a market long dominated by Wine Advocate, Wine Spectator and Decanter; Michelin owns Wine Advocate since 2019.

CULTURE.head: Champagne Barons de Rothschild releases two grand-cru single-vineyard cuvées
CULTURE.summ: The latest addition to the fast-developing Rothschild Champagne house is a pair of single-site expressions from grands crus in Avize and Cramant - and Patrick Schmitt MW gets an early taste
takeaways: Champagne Barons de Rothschild, founded in 2005 by three Rothschild family branches, launched Les Maladries and Le Bateau, blanc de blancs from 2021 harvests in Avize and Cramant.
Les Maladries is pure Chardonnay from a 0.3-hectare north-facing plot in Avize with vines over 80 years old, fermented half in concrete eggs and half in barrels, dosed at 3g/l.
Le Bateau comes from a 50-year-old Cramant vineyard on pure chalk soil, fermented and aged entirely in wood, with 2g/l dosage, creating a very dry style.

Cellar master Guillaume Lété said the single-harvest expressions, made only from maison-owned vineyards managed without herbicides, will continue annually with 2022 Champagnes expected to be richer due to warmer conditions.
These releases follow Le Grand Clos 2019, a single-plot Chardonnay from Vertus released June 2025 for the maison's 20th anniversary.

CULTURE.head: Fladgate Partnership expands beyond Port into Portuguese table wines
CULTURE.summ: The Fladgate Partnership is no longer a project just for Port - it now has wines from Bairrada, Vinhos Verdes and Dão alongside its Douro production. db explores a range informed by location as much as heritage
takeaways: The Fladgate Partnership, which owns Port houses Taylor's, Fonseca and Croft, has acquired still and sparkling wines from Bairrada, Vinhos Verdes and Dão since 2023.
The company will rebrand as Fladgate Family Wines to reflect its expanding portfolio beyond fortified wines across Portugal's diverse regions.
Douro red and white table wines called Silica and Croft Pink demonstrate the region's versatility; Croft Pink's light, refreshing style appeals beyond Port traditionalists.
Dão's Quinta de Bella blends native Touriga Nacional with international varieties including Pinot Noir, which shows age-worthiness comparable to Burgundy.

Bairrada's Quinta Colinas de São Lourenço produces sparkling wines and blends native and international grapes in Bordeaux-style assemblages; Vinhos Verdes properties focus on single-varietal Alvarinho and Loureiro that age beyond typical expectations.

CULTURE.head: Wine Hub integrates private collector marketplace to expand trading options
CULTURE.summ: Wine Hub, a leading wine business software owned by Wine Owners, has teamed up with management and trading exchange, The Wine Collector from Nickolls & Perks
takeaways: Wine Hub, a software platform owned Wine Owners, partnered with The Wine Collector, a management exchange run by Nickolls & Perks, to connect fine wine businesses with private collectors' inventory.
Wine Hub users can now bid on mature and rare wines stored in bonded warehouses, inspect them instantly, and transfer ownership between bond accounts without physical movement or duty complications.
The integration lets Wine Hub traders see collector offerings alongside their own stock, pricing, and margins in a single dashboard, similar to the existing Liv-Ex connection.
Will Gardener, managing director of Nickolls & Perks, said the move mirrors benefits from Wine Hub's established Liv-Ex integration and reduces double-selling risk through real-time stock control.
This follows Wine Hub's recent connection to LiveTrade, powered by Bordeaux Index, as fine wine software platforms build integrated ecosystems to deepen market liquidity and trading opportunities.

CULTURE.head: Mendoza winery restaurants claim two new Michelin stars in 2026 guide
CULTURE.summ: Two more winery restaurants in Mendoza have been awarded Michelin stars, further strengthening the region's reputation as one of South America's leading wine and gastronomy destinations. Winery and winemaker-led restaurants now account for almost half of Argentina's Michelin-starred establishments
takeaways: La VidA at Susana Balbo Winemaker's House and Cal at Sitio La Estocada earned Michelin stars in Argentina's third guide edition announced 13 July, bringing winery-restaurant total to six of fourteen starred establishments.
Cal, led by chef Enzo González Petra aged 28, also received a Michelin Green Star for sustainability commitment and seasonal sourcing; González Petra won the Young Chef Award for Argentina 2026.
Mendoza province now hosts nine of Argentina's fourteen Michelin-starred restaurants, cementing its status as South America's leading wine and gastronomy region.

Winery and winemaker-led restaurants account for nearly half of Argentina's Michelin-starred establishments since the guide's 2024 launch, with over a dozen more featured among recommended establishments.

CULTURE.head: English wine production surges 55% in 2025, driven by record heat and still-wine growth
CULTURE.summ: Following yesterday's 2026 rankings from The Real Review's 'Top Wineries of Great Britain' tasting, in which all of the top 30 wines hailed from England, Anthony Rose analyses the current state of UK wine
takeaways: UK wine production reached 124,377 hectolitres in 2025, equivalent to 16.5 million bottles—a 55% increase on 2024, with white wine output jumping 131%, according to Food Standards Agency data confirmed in March 2026.

Still wine production is accelerating, with Essex's Crouch Valley emerging as a centre for Pinot Noir and Chardonnay; Danbury Ridge Wine Estate announced a joint venture with Burgundy's Domaine Duroché, signalling international recognition of English quality.

The sector now comprises 1,158 registered vineyards across 4,357 hectares, employing over 10,000 people and valued at approximately £14 billion; UK wine exports grew 35% in 2024 to reach 9% of total sales.

CULTURE.head: Asia's drinks industry celebrates craftsmanship across Hong Kong, Seoul, Tokyo in June
CULTURE.summ: From Hong Kong to Tokyo, Asia's drinks industry came together in June through education, tasting experiences and major trade events, as mixologists, sommeliers and sake makers celebrated craftsmanship, and explored new opportunities for growth
takeaways: Stephan Berg, founder of The Bitter Truth bitters and liqueur producer, hosted a masterclass for Hong Kong bartenders at Bar Tincture as part of the brand's 20th-anniversary Asia Tour.

Wines of Hungary held its inaugural Bor Camp on 23–24 June at Mondrian Seoul Itaewon hotel, bringing together sommeliers, buyers and educators to explore Hungarian wine regions and grape varieties for South Korean market integration.
Tokyo's Sake Fair on 19–20 June drew 5,600 visitors to taste approximately 1,200 sakes from 45 Japanese prefectures, organised by the Japan Sake and Sochu Makers Association.
Lemsecco launched in China with Lemon and Blood Orange Spritz now stocked in over 400 Freshippo stores and premium supermarket chains including Ole, Apio and Citysuper.
The brand supported its Chinese launch with summer activations from May to August, including in-store tastings and partnership events with ASC Fine Wine.

SCIENCE.head: Half of Napa vineyards lost money last year; profitable ones pivot to hospitality
SCIENCE.summ: In WineBusiness Monthly’s annual Vineyard Survey Report, Napa Valley vintners voiced a lengthy list of concerns threatening their businesses: changing consumer preferences, succession planning, pests and diseases, labor shortages, rising farming costs, and increasingly strict regulations, to name a few.
takeaways: WineBusiness Monthly's annual survey found 50% of Napa Valley vineyards reported declining revenue and 51% saw profit drops in the past two vintages, with 2026 expected as a pivotal year.

The remaining 49% of profitable growers are succeeding through direct-to-consumer sales, flexible club memberships, and immersive tasting experiences rather than relying on walk-in tasting room traffic.

Napa's average tasting fee exceeds $75 and hotel costs surpass $400 per night, driving visitation down since 2022; successful wineries address this by lowering tasting prices and offering unique experiences.

Profitable producers like Chateau Montelena, Domaine Carneros, and Baldacci Family Vineyards prioritize member relationships, exclusive gatherings, and community-building to increase loyalty and referrals.
Grape oversupply has lowered prices, giving profitable wineries access to premium fruit from sought-after vineyards like Beckstoffer and Hyde that previously had years-long waitlists.

SCIENCE.head: Climate change brings grape sunburn to new regions; growers reverse leaf-removal practices
SCIENCE.summ: As climate change makes heat waves more prevalent, more producers have to combat sunburn in grapes, and the flabby, flat, rubbery notes they can bring to wine
takeaways: Grape sunburn is a stress response to excess light, UV radiation, and berry-surface heat; direct sun can push berries 22–27°F above air temperature, causing cellular damage and flavor degradation.

Chris Tanghe, MS, director of education for GuildSomm, identifies sunburned grapes by flattened fruit, oxidized bitterness, and notes of rubber, asphalt, and creosote; the damage is distinct from simple overripeness.
Joanna Gambetta, Ph.D., wine scientist at University of Newcastle, Australia, found that Chardonnay berries show symptoms within five minutes at surface temperatures of 104–109°F under high light.

Producers in Provence and Napa—including Chêne Bleu, Tyrrell's, and Matthiasson Wines—have abandoned mid-summer defoliation and now use kaolin clay sprays, earlier leaf removal, or canopy management to protect fruit.

Heat waves arriving earlier and further into traditionally cool regions are forcing vineyards worldwide to prioritize shade over sun exposure, fundamentally reversing decades of leaf-removal orthodoxy.

SCIENCE.head: France's heatwave cuts harvests across Champagne, Bordeaux, Burgundy
SCIENCE.summ: Weeks of drought are reducing yields and raising quality concerns, though weak global demand means shortages are far from certain
takeaways: Extreme heat and drought in France are reducing grape yields significantly in major wine regions, with Champagne expecting 10% lower production and Bordeaux potentially facing 20% or greater declines.
High temperatures accelerate ripening and raise alcohol levels, risking grape imbalance; some estates are irrigating despite AOC restrictions to save vines as ground temperatures approach 50°C.

French regions are harvesting earlier than ever—Champagne around August 15, potentially the earliest on record—adapting to climate change that has steadily shifted the growing cycle earlier over the past decade.
Global wine shortages are unlikely due to weak demand and excess inventory, but premium Burgundy whites and Sauternes face tighter supplies as Pinot Noir and entry-level Bordeaux remain oversupplied.
Quality remains uncertain until grapes are tasted; small berry size and potential incomplete phenolic maturation could compromise wines despite apparent ripeness if drought persists.

SCIENCE.head: Heat-Tolerant Mourvèdre Struggles as Southern France Temperatures Rise
SCIENCE.summ: In a warming climate, even one of the world's most heat-tolerant grape varieties is reaching its limits
takeaways: Mourvèdre, traditionally grown in Bandol on France's southern coast, was chosen for its ability to ripen in hot climates, but rising temperatures and drought are now pushing it beyond sustainable limits.
Even heat-loving Mourvèdre requires moderation: the saying 'head in the sun, feet in the sea' reflects that coastal and high-altitude sites moderate extreme heat that would otherwise damage grapes.
Producers including Jean-François Ott at Château Romassan and the Gueissards at Domaine Gueissard are adapting canopy management and planting shade trees to prevent sunburn and slow ripening cycles.

Drought poses a greater threat than heat alone: Jumilla's surface area halved in 20 years as southern parcels were abandoned, and irrigation—once taboo for dry-farmed Bandol—is now considered essential for vine survival.
Bandol's rosé production, which accounts for 75 percent of output, may decline as cooler sites traditionally reserved for rosé are converted to red wine as growers shift to climate-resilient parcels.

SCIENCE.head: Organic Sauvignon Blanc gaining ground despite challenging vineyard conditions
SCIENCE.summ: From fruity and tropical to stony and herbal, there's a Sauvignon style for everyone, if you know where to look. These organic examples are perfect for summer
takeaways: Sauvignon Blanc is difficult to grow organically because the vine is sensitive to powdery and downy mildew, which require frequent fungicide treatment in humid years.
Jonathan Pabiot, a biodynamic winemaker in Pouilly-Fumé, says copper-based fungicides—the main organic option—harm the vine's flowers, forcing growers to find alternative solutions.

Andreas Tscheppe farms Sauvignon Blanc biodynamically at 500m altitude in south Styria, Austria, using natural yeasts and minimal sulphur to produce textured, wildflower-forward wines aged two years in large barrels.
Patricia and Luc Bertoni of Domaine les Eminades in Languedoc credit nearly 20 years of organic farming and the region's mild climate with building disease resilience in their vines.
Despite challenges, organic Sauvignon Blanc production is expanding as winemakers worldwide develop sustainable methods to manage disease while preserving the grape's aromatic complexity.

SCIENCE.head: Ten lesser-known Scotch brands offer diverse styles beyond mainstream names
SCIENCE.summ: Scotch is a simple beverage at its core. The whisky consists of three ingredients: barley, water, and yeast. The elements beyond this base are significantly more complex.
takeaways: VinePair curated ten underrated Scotch brands spanning blended and single-malt styles to challenge the misconception that all Scotch is heavily peated.
Monkey Shoulder, a blended Speyside Scotch, dominates cocktail bars for its balanced honey, vanilla, and citrus profile suited to mixed drinks.
Glenglassaugh's Sandend and Bruichladdich's The Classic Laddie are unpeated or lightly peated Islay options that introduce novices to the region without overwhelming smoke.
Dr. Rachel Barrie, master blender at Glendronach, Glenglassaugh, and Benriach, crafts Scotches emphasizing sense of place; her Glendronach 12 Year matures in sherry casks.
Glengoyne's White Oak, Compass Box's Artist Blend, Mortlach 12 Year, and Auchentoshan American Oak represent overlooked regional styles from Speyside and Lowland distilleries.

SCIENCE.head: Osaka scientists breed high-ornithine yeast for functional craft beer
SCIENCE.summ: Researchers in Japan say they have developed a naturally derived yeast strain capable of producing significantly higher levels of ornithine, an amino acid commonly used in health supplements, in a bid to tap into the trend for value-added fermented drinks
takeaways: Researchers led by Hiroshi Takagi at Osaka universities developed ADHorn49, a yeast strain producing nine times more ornithine than standard brewer's yeast, using conventional breeding rather than genetic modification.

Ornithine is an amino acid marketed in health supplements for reducing fatigue and improving sleep; the new yeast allows brewers to create value-added beers containing the compound without gene editing.

Laboratory tests showed ADHorn49 fermented brewing wort normally while releasing 7.0mg ornithine per litre; 10 Fields Factory brewed a prototype ale with 19.0mg per litre versus 6.1mg in standard beer.
The team developed ADHorn49 from naturally occurring yeast via chemical mutagenesis and selective breeding, appealing to consumers seeking non-genetically modified products.

SCIENCE.head: Paris bans public drinking as record heat strains hospitals
SCIENCE.summ: Paris again banned public drinking this weekend in response to the record-breaking heatwave engulfing parts of Europe. First responders and hospitals are already fighting a weather-induced influx of patients, Paris police chief Patrice Faure says, and the drinking ban is a move to prevent further hospitalizations, according to...
takeaways: Paris police chief Patrice Faure suspended public alcohol consumption from noon Friday through 7 a.m. Saturday, repeating the ban Saturday into Sunday, citing heat-related hospitalizations.
Separate restrictions prohibit selling or purchasing takeaway alcohol from 6 p.m. Friday to 7 a.m. Saturday and again Saturday evening to Sunday morning; bars and restaurants operate normally.
Paris hospitals recorded four times more cardiac arrests than typical on Wednesday and Thursday, according to health minister Stéphanie Rist, prompting the emergency measure.
Faure also suspended sporting events and postponed Paris Pride from June to September due to heat; he warns alcohol intensifies heat exhaustion but says violators face fines, not detention.
This marks the second public drinking ban in a week—France also prohibited alcohol at Fête de la Musique music festival last weekend despite cleanup finding discarded bottles.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Exploring speciality coffee and its parallels with fine wine: a visit to Fazenda Mió in Brazil to catch the harvest
NEWSLETTERS.summ: There are many parallels between speciality coffee and wine, and that’s one of the reasons I was really excited about
NEWSLETTERS.author: Jamie Goode
takeaways: Brazil produces 3.5 million tonnes of coffee annually, about one-third of global production, with Minas Gerais as the heart of production.
Fazenda Mió spans over 2,000 hectares with 665 hectares of coffee plants; the farm employs precision agriculture mapping to match coffee varieties to specific plots.
Coffee bushes yield after 3 years, are pruned every few years, have a 20-year active lifespan, and Mió maintains bushes over 70 years old.
Unlike wine (fermented juice), coffee processing extracts seeds from the cherry; fermentation plays a less direct role in coffee flavor than in wine.
Fazenda Mió deliberately cultivates early, medium, and late coffee varieties to spread the May-to-August harvest; the dry season (June-August) provides ideal conditions for drying.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Best Wine-Related Click Bait AI Can Buy
NEWSLETTERS.summ: The allure of clickbait and reasons to support the real writers
NEWSLETTERS.author: Tom Wark
takeaways: An article titled "The Absolute Best Winery in Every U.S. State" generated more clicks than any other story in a curated wine newsletter throughout an entire year.
Wine publications increasingly rely on SEO-optimized clickbait headlines and AI-generated content to drive engagement.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Wine News: What I’m Reading the Week of 7/12/26
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Hello and welcome to my weekly roundup of the wine stories that I find of interest on the web. I
NEWSLETTERS.author: Alder Yarrow

NEWSLETTERS.head: Notable Releases from Troon Vineyard
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Notes on a Vermentino and two Syrahs, one with some bottle age
NEWSLETTERS.author: Meg Maker
takeaways: Troon Vineyard released 2025 Vermentino and 2023 Syrah from Oregon's Applegate Valley.
Troon Vineyard farms biodynamic and regenerative organic, using only ambient cultures for fermentation and minimal sulfites.
Troon ages wines in neutral barrels, concrete, qvevri, and stainless steel—never new oak.
Applegate Valley's high latitude, overnight temperature drops from Siskiyou Mountains, and alluvial soils suit Mediterranean grapes.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Is Red Wine Losing Its Grip?
NEWSLETTERS.summ: The fading appetite for structured reds, and what it says about the way we drink now
NEWSLETTERS.author: George Nordahl
takeaways: An OIV 2023 study shows sustained red wine consumption decline relative to white and rosé since 2007, with earlier peaks around 2000 in Germany, France, Italy, and Spain.
French beef consumption fell 13% and pork 11% between 2000-2021, while poultry rose 31%, creating fewer occasions for structured, tannic red wines.
Modern French meals are shorter, less formal, and increasingly eaten away from the family table, reducing traditional settings for wine consumption.
Contemporary diets feature more sushi, Thai curries, Mediterranean mezze, and lighter preparations that pair more naturally with white, rosé, and sparkling wines than structured reds.
Wine increasingly consumed separately from meals—as social beverages before/after dinner or while watching television—where tannic reds often taste drying and heavy rather than complementary.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Friday 5 #186
NEWSLETTERS.summ: 4 lush white wines under £10, this month’s cookbook giveaway and where to stop for a bite that isn't Greggs
NEWSLETTERS.author: Fiona Beckett

NEWSLETTERS.head: Mountains and Magliocco
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Calabria might be known as the land of wine, but it depends where you go
NEWSLETTERS.author: Simon J. Woolf
takeaways: Casa Comerci, run by sisters Rosa and Michela Comerci near Nicotera, produces organic wines from Magliocco Canino and Greco Bianco on 15 hectares certified organic since 2009.
Magliocco Canino, possibly related to Sangiovese, retains high acidity and ripens extremely late—Casa Comerci's 2017 rosé was harvested in early December.
Casa Comerci ferments and ages wines exclusively in stainless steel tanks, never barrels, producing fruit-focused wines with plummy perfumes.
Calabria's Cirò region, dominated by large producer Librandi, remains far more commercially prominent than the Tyrrhenian coastal areas despite being 200km away.

NEWSLETTERS.head: Andrew Jefford: the master of wine writing
NEWSLETTERS.summ: "He’s like a learned clergyman from the 19th century, composing poetry, cataloguing wines, observing nature and taking an interest in everything around him"
NEWSLETTERS.author: Henry Jeffreys
takeaways: Andrew Jefford's book 'The New France' (2004) is now 20 years old and remains his last in-depth wine book due to poor publishing economics.
A new collection of Jefford's essays, 'Drinking with the Valkyries', has been published by Academie du Vin.
Jefford argues wine scoring systems are 'foolish' and 'philosophically untenable', and that grape variety focus oversimplifies wine appreciation.
Jefford studied creative writing at UEA alongside Kazuo Ishiguro and is noted for poetic, intellectually rigorous wine writing comparable to Victoria Moore.

NEWSLETTERS.head: My €4.50 plastic-bottle wine shame
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Or why local cheap plonk can be perfectly good enough in holiday sunshine. Plus: the best places I've eaten in Greece in the past fortnight
NEWSLETTERS.author: Andy Neather
takeaways: Author purchased local Lefkadan white and rosé wines in plastic litre bottles for €4.50 and €4.70 on Greek islands.
Taverna house whites and rosés in Greece cost around €7 per litre carafe; glass-bottled local Lefkadan wine costs €13.
Local Greek whites are typically light (11-12.5% ABV), fresh, citrussy with herbal notes, likely Roditis or Robola grapes.
Context—setting, company, food, and mood—matters as much as wine quality; holiday wines often taste worse at home despite unchanged content.

NEWSLETTERS.head: The Ghosts of California Vino
NEWSLETTERS.summ: Why is America’s greatest wine monument just lying there dormant?
NEWSLETTERS.author: Robert Camuto
takeaways: Italian Swiss Colony, founded in 1881 by Genovese immigrant Andrea Sbarbaro near Cloverdale, Sonoma County, was California's most important winery before Napa's rise and the Judgment of Paris.
Sbarbaro built the world's largest wine cistern holding 500,000 gallons and planted Italian varietals including Nebbiolo, Grignolino, Barbera, and Sangiovese in the colony's vineyards.

Pre-Prohibition, Italian Swiss Colony produced millions of gallons from nine California wineries and exported globally, hiring French Champagne master Charles Jadeau in 1909 to make Golden State Extra Dry Champagne.

Prohibition shifted the colony from wine production to selling grapes and dried grape bricks for home use; post-Prohibition, Americans demanded high-alcohol sweet wines over Sbarbaro's dry European-style wines, causing the market to shrink.
E & J Gallo purchased Italian Swiss Colony in 2015; today it produces mostly Gallo brands like Orin Swift, Ravenswood, and Souverain in a fraction of its original space.
